one_line_takeaway: A broad, lifecycle-organized survey of LLM security, privacy, misuse, and mitigation that is valuable as a taxonomy and reading map, but contributes no original experiments—every number in it comes from a cited study.
key_finding: The security risks of LLMs are best understood along the model lifecycle: model-based, training-time, and inference-time. Cross-cutting empirical observations the survey highlights (all drawn from cited works, not the authors' own runs): (a) memorization/leakage scales with model size and is non-monotonic across model families; (b) instruction-tuned models exhibit 'inverse scaling'—larger models are MORE susceptible to data poisoning; (c) jailbreak prompts can reach near-total success rates on deployed chatbots; (d) LLM-generated code is frequently insecure and existing code-watermarking/detection methods (motivating SWEET) often break; and (e) black-box AI-text detectors are more practical than white-box ones because most deployed LLMs expose no token probabilities. The paper's thesis: defenses must be layered (pre-processing, training-time, inference-time) and paired with policy and user education, since no single mitigation is sufficient.
core_contribution: A survey (systematization-of-knowledge) paper by Abdali et al. (Microsoft / UC Riverside) that organizes the LLM security and privacy literature into a coherent taxonomy. Its own contribution is not a new attack, defense, or experiment, but the organizing framework: (1) a glossary of ~18 key terms (Table 1); (2) a three-way classification of adversarial vulnerabilities into Model-based (extraction, imitation/leeching), Training-time (data poisoning, backdoor), and Inference-time (paraphrasing, spoofing, prompt injection/leaking, jailbreaking); (3) a separate axis of 'security & privacy concerns' (PII/sensitive-info leakage, memorization, insecure LLM-generated code); (4) a survey of 'misuse risks' (factual inconsistency/hallucination, toxicity/discrimination, copyright/plagiarism, misinformation); and (5) a catalog of mitigation strategies (model editing, red/blue/green/purple teaming, watermarking, and AI-generated-text detection via white-box/black-box/zero-shot/retrieval/feature-based methods), each with noted limitations, plus future-research directions. Summary Tables 2-4 tabulate selected poisoning, prompt-injection, and jailbreak works.
threat_model: The paper does not adopt a single threat model; as a survey it aggregates many. Adversary capabilities range across the LLM lifecycle: black-box API query access (model extraction/leeching, PII probing, jailbreaking, prompt injection), white-box access held by model providers (worst-case PII reconstruction), training-data control (data poisoning, backdoor trigger insertion), and control of external content sources consumed by LLM-integrated apps/agents (indirect prompt injection via websites). Assets at risk: training-data confidentiality (PII, proprietary code/secrets), model IP/parameters, output integrity and safety alignment, and downstream applications/agents. It also frames misuse where the LLM itself is the attacker's tool (generating malware/phishing, misinformation, toxic content, plagiarism).
limitations: As acknowledged/implied by the paper: (1) it is a rapidly-dating narrative survey with no original evaluation, so all quantitative claims are second-hand and not independently reproduced; (2) coverage is explicitly 'non-exhaustive'; (3) the taxonomy categories overlap (e.g., prompt leaking spans privacy leakage and inference-time attack), and the same works recur across sections; (4) it enumerates the well-known challenges of security-evaluating code generation—non-reproducibility of black-box outputs, difficulty generating statistically valid corpora, synthetic-scenario unrealism, prompt sensitivity, language-dependent security, and the moving target of 'secure practice'—without resolving them; (5) mitigations are described qualitatively with trade-offs but no head-to-head effectiveness comparison.

datasets_used: None of the authors' own; the survey references datasets/benchmarks from cited works, e.g. AttaQ (adversarial harmful-question dataset), SUMMEDITS (factual-consistency benchmark for summarization), and FIB (Factual Inconsistency Benchmark)

Cited models discussed as targets/examples: OPT, LLaMA, Mistral, PaLM, RoBERTa-large, XLNET-large, BERT-large, GPT-NEO-1.3B, GPT-3, GPT-4, ChatGPT, New Bing, Bard, Claude, GitHub Copilot (Codex), Alpaca/Vicuna/Koala (imitation)
quantitative_results: Fraction of training data memorized: 0.1%-1% (from Nasr et al., generating 1 billion output tokens) — cited work, not authors' own
Unique extractable 50-token strings range from hundreds of thousands (OPT) to millions (LLaMA, Mistral) — cited (Nasr et al.)
~100 poisoned examples suffice to distort instruction-tuned model outputs, with an 'inverse scaling' effect (larger models more susceptible) — cited (Wan et al., 2023)
Jailbreak attack success rate as high as 0.99 on ChatGPT and GPT-4 — cited (Shen et al., 2023)
GPT-4 is the best model on SUMMEDITS but still inferior to human factual-consistency performance — cited (Laban et al., 2023)
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines run by the authors. Comparative statements are qualitative summaries of cited works, e.g. NOTABLE reported to achieve higher ASR than BToP and BadPrompt across three classification tasks; SWEET code-watermarking vs. failing standard watermarking/detection; AI-text detectors GPTZero vs. ZeroGPT vs. OpenAI detector vs. white-box HowkGPT compared narratively
